plainMeaning: The monks say: we get the food we need, yet in doing so we harm no one — just as bees feed from flowers without damaging them.
recitation: vayaṁ ca vittiṁ labbhāmo, na ya koi uvahammai | ahāgaḍesu riyante, pupphesu bhamarā jahā ||

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit vayaṁ, 1st-person plural nominative, from Sanskrit vayam ("we").
wordByWord.notes: Marks the shift into the monks' own first-person voice — they speak of themselves.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit ca = Sanskrit ca, connective particle.
wordByWord.notes: Here "too" — like the bee, the monk also gains sustenance.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit vitti (accusative vittiṁ) = Sanskrit vṛtti (√vṛt, "to subsist, carry on"), "means of living."
wordByWord.notes: Not wealth, but the bare daily maintenance a mendicant may rightfully seek.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit labbhāmo, 1st-person plural present, from √labh (Sanskrit labhāmahe), "we receive."
Prakrit ya = Sanskrit ca, connective; with na forms na ya, "and not."
wordByWord.notes: na ya together = "and yet no…", stressing the reciprocity of the two clauses.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit koi = Sanskrit kaścit, indefinite pronoun, "someone, any."
wordByWord.notes: Deliberately unrestricted — neither giver, nor household, nor any creature.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit uvahammai, 3rd-person singular passive, from upa-√han (Sanskrit upahanyate), "is struck down, damaged."
wordByWord.notes: Passive: the focus is on the absence of any victim, not on an agent.

wordByWord.gloss: among (food) made-as-it-is, i.e. already prepared for the household's own use
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit ahāgaḍa (locative plural ahāgaḍesu) = Sanskrit yathākṛta, "as (already) made/done."
wordByWord.notes: Technical term for faultless alms not cooked or arranged for the monk's sake; locative "in the case of / among such food."

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit riyante, present participle from √rī/√ṛ (cf. Sanskrit īyante / caranti), "moving, going on the alms-round."
wordByWord.notes: The gocarī (alms-walk) itself; participle qualifying the unspoken "we / the monks."

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit puppha (locative plural pupphesu) = Sanskrit puṣpa, "flower."
wordByWord.notes: Locative parallels ahāgaḍesu — flowers are to bees what ready-made food is to monks.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit bhamara (nominative plural bhamarā) = Sanskrit bhramara, "bee" (one who hovers/roams, √bhram).
Prakrit jahā = Sanskrit yathā, particle of comparison.
wordByWord.notes: Closes the simile clause; the correlative "so" is left understood.

literalTranslation: And we too obtain our livelihood, and no one is harmed; ranging among food already made (for others' use), as bees among the flowers.
idiomaticTranslation: "We too win the sustenance we need, and yet by it no living being is hurt — for we range only among food made ready of itself, exactly as bees range among the flowers."

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Where the previous verse described the true śramaṇas from the outside, this verse hands the microphone to the monks themselves: vayaṁ — "we." It is their own vow spoken aloud, and that change of voice is the verse's whole force. They state two things that must hold together: vittiṁ labbhāmo, "we do obtain our livelihood," and na ya koi uvahammai, "and yet not anyone is injured." The mendicant is not a starving ascetic who refuses sustenance; he eats, he survives — but the surviving must cost no creature anything. The hinge that makes both clauses true at once is the technical word ahāgaḍa (yathākṛta): the monk ranges (riyante) only among food prepared by the householder for the household's own use, never cooked, bought, or set aside for him. Because nothing is produced on his account, no fire is lit, no being killed, no extra burden laid on the giver — so "no one is harmed" is literal, not pious overstatement. The closing simile, pupphesu bhamarā jahā, locks the two locatives together: ahāgaḍesu ("among ready-made food") answers pupphesu ("among flowers"). The bee draws what sustains it from a blossom that is left whole, unwithered, undiminished; the monk draws his meal from a home left exactly as it was. The verse thus turns the chapter's image into a first-person rule of conduct — the reciter is made to promise it in his own mouth.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse compresses the eṣaṇā-samiti — carefulness in seeking food — into a single ethical equation: legitimate self-maintenance must equal zero net harm. yathākṛta/ahāgaḍa alms are the doctrinal safeguard of ahiṁsā at the dinner-plate: by accepting only food already existing for others, the monk avoids ārambha (the violence latent in cooking and procuring) and severs himself from any causal chain of killing done "for his sake." na ya koi uvahammai widens this beyond the obvious: not only the flower/giver, but no being whatever in the chain of preparation is struck. Read together, vittiṁ labbhāmo and the harmlessness clause refuse a false choice between asceticism and non-violence — Jain monastic ethics demands both at once: one lives, and one lives harmlessly.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited in the first person, this verse asks the practitioner to test his own consumption against the bee's standard: do I take only what already exists, leaving the source undiminished? For a layperson it translates into mindful, non-extractive living — meeting genuine needs without generating fresh harm, demanding nothing be made or destroyed merely on one's own account, and being content with what is freely available rather than what must be specially arranged.

crossReferences.text: Daśavaikālika 1.2–1.3 (the bee/bird simile that this verse voices in the first person)
Daśavaikālika 5 (Piṇḍeṣaṇā), on faultless seeking of alms and yathākṛta food

sources.title: Daśavaikālika (Dashavaikalika) — encyclopedic entry: 2nd Mūlasūtra, ascribed to Śayyambhava, ten chapters
sources.consultedFor: Authorship, canonical status, structure, and Ardhamāgadhī composition of the Daśavaikālika

sources.title: Yathākṛta (यथाकृत) — Apte, Monier-Williams, Cappeller & Cologne Sanskrit dictionaries
sources.consultedFor: Meaning of ahāgaḍa = yathākṛta, "made/done as per rule or custom; already prepared" — the alms term in pāda c

sources.title: Vṛtti (वृत्ति) — Cologne Sanskrit dictionaries entry
sources.consultedFor: Sense of vittiṁ = vṛtti, "means of subsistence, livelihood" (√vṛt)

notes: Verse = Daśavaikālika 1.4 (Ardhamāgadhī). Book orthography preserved verbatim (vittiṁ, uvahammai, ahāgaḍesu, riyante, pupphesu, sāhūṇo-family spellings) and not normalized. uvahammai = Sanskrit upahanyate (passive). koi (= kaścit) appears in some editions as ko vi — a minor variant not affecting sense. riyante is read as a present participle of motion (√rī/√ṛ); some commentators gloss it simply as "wandering on gocarī." The crux ahāgaḍa is the technical yathākṛta — food already made for the householder's own use, not for the monk — which is what reconciles "we obtain food" with "no one is harmed"; this reading follows the standard Śvetāmbara commentarial tradition and the sutra's own Piṇḍeṣaṇā chapter.
